Introduction
1.1 Objective
This method of statement is prepared prior to performing the activity for Commissioning Level 2A of MV Switchgear after finish installation. The purpose of this work method statement is to ensure that all panels is carried out in proper, control condition and safe environment. All of the personnel involve in this activity shall read and understand fully this document and agree to obey and follow strictly before starting works.
1.2 Scope of Work
Commissioning Level 2 (Cx L2) of MV Switchgear. Installation verification (QA/QC) and static testing.
1.3 Cx Organization Chart
1.4 Work Flow
`
References
2.1 Asbuilt Drawing
2.2 Approval Material
Precondition
3.1 Safety Information Personal
TBA
3.2 Safety Work
Prior to start of work, risk assessment highlighting critical safety aspect to the work shall be briefed to workers.
Ensure that all personnel at site have passed on-line induction exam.
Ensure work skill for moving work.
Ensure that all personnel at site have attended the Safety course
Ensure that all tools and equipment have been inspected.
Housekeeping in to be done during and after work.
Personal Protective Equipment:
Safety Helmet
Protective Clothing
Electric Detectors Warning Device
Safety Shoes
Safety Gloves
3.3 Risk & Mitigation Risk
3.4 LOTO (Lock Out Tag Out)
Make sure the voltage source to the incoming side is still o

How to use this document
This document outlines the Modbus map for the PM8000 device and includes the tags that can be used to reference quantities in custom web pages. There is both a high-level and a detailed view of the Modbus map in this document. For more information on the contents of fields in the detailed Modbus map, see the additional worksheets with the corresponding field names.

Modbus Map Section | Start Address | End Address | Comments
Generic data | 0 | 65535 | Excludes 52001-59999 inclusive for other sections described below
External Control | 52001 | 53000 | Includes external pulse, external boolean, and external numeric. The registers shown in the detailed Modbus Map are all of the possible registers for this section. At any given time these registers may or may not be active.
Data Recorders | 53001 | 53153
Enumerated Registers | 54001 | 56000 | The registers shown in the detailed Modbus Map are only a small subset of the registers contained in this section.
Numeric Registers | 56001 | 59999 | The registers shown in the detailed Modbus Map are only a small subset of the registers contained in this section.
Modbus Slave Modules | 0 | 65535 | Modbus addresses used through Modbus slave module cannot conflict with addresses in the generic data section but will automatically overwrite any data presented in the external control, data recorder, enumerated register and numeric register sections.

Building Management System 
Page 1 of 10 
Version: 1.5 
 
Global Design Standards 
Equinix Confidential 
Date: 2020-07-02 
GLOBAL IBX STANDARD: 
EBCS-1: Data Center Operations System 
 
 
Content Approval: Craig Pennington, Rick Matteazzi, Diraj Bamola 
Distribution: Ops Engineering, Design Engineering, Product Management, Site Ops, Construction  
 
 
History: 
 
Application of Standards 
Standards Documents contain requirements that are to be complied with on a global basis. However, it is 
recognized that market, operational and technical requirements will change over time, so input from regional 
engineering teams and other stakeholders is encouraged. Also, as regional teams work with outside engineering 
firms, it should be emphasized that those firms are responsible to provide designs and detailed construction 
documents that are fully compliant with all applicable codes, and that conflicts between Equinix standards and 
applicable codes are to be brought to our attention. 
 
Purpose 
The Data Center Operations System (DCOS) shall be a layered and networked architecture of controllers and 
devices with functionality for continuous operation and monitoring of building mechanical and electrical systems. 
In this document DCOS refers to all local mechanical and electrical control and monitoring systems which may 
also locally be called Building Management Systems (BMS), Energy Management Systems (EMS), Electrical 
Power Monitoring Systems (EPMS), Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) or optimization systems.  
 
This design standard shall be used as the basis of design requirements to aid the engineer in BMS system design 
for International Business Exchange (IBX). 
 
Applicability 
This standard establishes DCOS system architecture and functional criteria for all Greenfield sites as well as site 
expansions or other projects with a total project cost >USD15M. Site expansions or other projects at existing sites 
where the total project cost is <USD15M m

Branch Circuit Monitoring 
Page 1 of 3 
Version: 0.75 
 
Global Design Standards 
Equinix Confidential 
Date:  Apr. 2, 2015 
 
GLOBAL IBX STANDARD: 
EES-10:  Branch Circuit Monitoring  
 
 
Content Approval: Rick Matteazzi 
Distribution: Ops Engineering, Design Engineering, Product Management, Site Ops, Construction  
Contributors: Ernest Holloway, Paul Crouch, Rajesh Byre Gowda, Derrek Telkamp 
 
History: 
 
Application of Standards 
Standards Documents contain requirements that are to be complied with on a global basis.  However, it 
is recognized that market, operational and technical requirements will change over time, so input from 
regional engineering teams and other stakeholders is encouraged.  Also, as regional teams work with 
outside engineering firms, it should be emphasized that those firms are responsible to provide designs 
and detailed construction documents that are fully compliant with all applicable codes, and that conflicts 
between Equinix standards and applicable codes are to be brought to our attention. 
 
Purpose 
This document defines the hardware selection to be used in Branch Circuit Monitoring systems (BCM) 
at all Equinix IBXs. 
 
Applicability 
This document applies to all Equinix IBX facilities and is to be used as reference for design elements of 
BCM and their selection and implementation.  It is supplementary to other applicable standards 
including, but not limited to, topology,  and global procurement. 
 
1.0   RESPONSIBILITIES: 
Global Operations Engineering shall maintain this standard with the assistance of the BCM stake 
holders. 
It is the responsibility of all Operations Engineering, Design Engineering, and Construction personnel to 
comply with this policy.   
It is the responsibility of the Manager Facilities Operations and/or Assistant Manager Facilities 
Operations to ensure that all Site Operations Engineering personnel are aware, understand, and have 
been trained on the implementation of this policy and its requirem

---
EGS-2 Global Naming Standard 
Page 1 of 11 
Version: 0.09 
 
Global Design Standards 
Equinix Confidential 
Date: 2021-11-17 
GLOBAL IBX STANDARD: 
EGS-2: Equipment and DCOS Naming 
 
 
Content Approval:  
Distribution: Ops Engineering, Design Engineering, Product Management, Site Ops, Construction  
 
 
History: 
 
Application of Standards 
Standards Documents contain requirements that are to be complied with on a global basis. However, it is 
recognized that market, operational and technical requirements will change over time, so input from regional 
engineering teams and other stakeholders is encouraged. Also, as regional teams work with outside engineering 
firms, it should be emphasized that those firms are responsible to provide designs and detailed construction 
documents that are fully compliant with all applicable codes, and that conflicts between Equinix standards and 
applicable codes are to be brought to our attention. 
 
Purpose 
This document defines equipment, instrument, and tag naming conventions. The equipment name is used on 
design and construction documents, on equipment labels and in the DCOS system. Instrument and tag names 
are used on P&ID drawings, location drawings, instrument and wiring labels, instrument data sheets, equipment 
labels, equipment data sheets, instrument indexes, equipment indexes, field drawings, vendor drawings, and 
equipment/system sequence of operations. This documents also defines tag rules for PLC or other control 
devices. 
 
This document is not intended to be fully prescriptive for the tagging of internal PLC or controller points. PLC 
internal, program scope, variables such as a timer preset do not need to strictly follow these requirements unless 
the point is to be displayed at the DCOS, in which case it should conform to this standard as closely as possible. 
 
This design standard shall be used in conjunction with Standards EBCS-1 and EBCS-3 as the basis of design 
requirements to aid the engineer in system

The following test procedure prepared as a guideline for testing and commissioning.
This is to be enhanced and developed by the Contractor. Additional testing and commissioning requirements may be proposed by the Contractor as appropriate and agreed with the Project M&E Engineer and Independent Testing and Commissioning Engineer.
Introduction including:
Objective
Scope of Work
Cx Orchard and response (Commissioning Organization Chart & Responsibility)
Workflow Chart
Reference Standards
Reference Standards should be international Standards for Testing Commissioning (NEBB, CIBSE, BSRIA, etc.). These standards should be adhered to throughout the testing and commissioning process.
Precondition
This section contains information about information health / safety personnel, tools, equipment, and necessary conditions for procedure job.
Health/ Safety Information Personal
Safety Work
Risk & Mitigation Risk
LOTO (Lock Out Tag Out) for CxL3
Test Instrumentation / Tools Required
All instrumentation used for the testing shall come complete with up to date (with at least 6 months/ 1 year validity) calibration certificates that are traceable to international standards.

Equipment
Brand
Type

Equipment Name
Location
Model

Pre – Commissioning
Pre-commissioning is the preparation of prerequisite documents, visual checks needed to complete before Test Procedures Commissioning is carried out Ex QAQC report, completion installation.
Test Procedures
The methodology in the test will be explained in this subtitle. All tests must be made and explained in accordance with international standards, adjust to the test to be carried out including Passing Criteria.
Appendix
Test Sheet (including passing Criteria)
Certificate Calibration
Approval Material
Datasheet Equipme

content:

---
DCOS Interface Sign-off
For
TRANSFORMER
ISSUE CONTROL RECORD
Customer:	Equinix
Project:	JK1-2 DCOS Interface Sign-off
This table provides a record of the issues of the DCOS Interface sign-off. Signature of the document below constitutes acceptance of the latest issue shown. The accepted document provides the basis for all work undertaken by Orise.
CONTENTS
General Notes – Sign Off
This document gathers the final DCOS points list and associated manual from each MCE vendor/GC for Equinix JK1-2. Signing off this document signifies that the information provided for the equipment and/or systems is accurate.
The information provided has been checked by parties having direct knowledge of the equipment and/or systems as indicated by the sign-off provided below.
Please be aware that any incomplete or inaccurate information shared for development will lead to rework or adversely affect the project's schedule.
Refer to the Sections below for required submissions. In each section, MCE/GC to sign-off to indicate compliance with the requirement.
Introduction
Note the following requirement for DCOS sign-off document:
Approved Network Switches
Only approved Moxa switches are permitted.
Unauthorized network extensions outside the equipment are strictly prohibited.
Approved Models: Moxa EDS-205 or EDS-208
Approved Serial-to-Ethernet Gateway
Only approved Serial-to-Ethernet Gateways may be used.
Reference: See Appendix 5 – Serial to Ethernet Gateway
Factory Acceptance Testing (FAT)

No deviations from the approved configuration are allowed.

topic: CORE AGI Skill V4 — Layer 1 Design
content: CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md is the active skill file at C:\Users\rnvgg\.claude-skills\CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md. Layer 1 only — covers system identity, owner context, infrastructure addresses, discovery protocol, brain map, component map, tool universe, local PC rules, failure recovery, session end, and hard rules. Layer 2 (behavior pipeline) is separate and not yet built. Key design principle: Layer 1 teaches CORE how to discover itself via session_start + system_map, never hardcodes component lists. 10-year proof by design.

topic: CORE Behavior Pipeline — Layer 2 Design
content: Behavior Pipeline concept: Layer 2 of the CORE skill. Two tracks — Knowledge Track (classify, verify gate, research, confidence check, answer, self-audit) and Execution Track (decompose, plan review, resource check, execute, verify, log). Research node queries ALL brain tables: knowledge_base, mistakes, sessions, changelog, evolution_queue, pattern_frequency + web search. Default behavior seeded as KB rows with sequence field so injection renders as ordered pipeline. Each table has a defined purpose in the pipeline. Self-audit at session end writes new KB entries for assumption-risk patterns.

topic: Skill and repo cleanup — March 2026 session
content: Files deleted this session as part of cleanup audit: LOCAL: CORE_AGI_SKILL.md, CORE_AGI_SKILL_v2.md, CORE_v5_plan.md, master_prompt.md, Boot.txt, CORE_SYSTEM_MAP.md (all stale/superseded). GITHUB: docs/TASK2_SPLIT_PLAN.md (task complete), docs/ZAPIER_SKILL_GRAPH.md (duplicate of local copy), core_tools_test.py (stale), patches/evo_293_20260313_203916.patch (applied and dead). Active skill files remaining: CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md (active), CORE_AGI_SKILL_v3.md (backward reference), CORE_v6_plan.md (active plan).

topic: Safe patch pattern for core_tools.py and core_train.py
content: When patching core_tools.py or core_train.py, always check if the target block contains em-dashes, ampersands, or complex string literals. If yes: fetch full file via Python urllib gh_get, edit in memory with string replace inside a .py script written via Desktop Commander:write_file, then push via gh_push. Never use multi_patch or gh_search_replace on these files for complex blocks. Never use PowerShell -Command for multi-line Python strings. Correct pattern: write_file(patch.py) then python.exe patch.py. Validated 2026-03-14 during mine_kb deprecation.

topic: backlog table dropped -- mine_kb deprecated 2026-03-14
content: backlog table is dropped as of 2026-03-14 (v6.1.2). mine_kb, get_backlog, backlog_update are all deprecated and stubbed to return {deprecated: true}. Removed from TOOLS registry. run_kb_mining stubbed to no-op in core_train.py. background_researcher auto-trigger block removed. Cold processor pipeline via evolution_queue fully replaces KB mining. Do not attempt to query the backlog table -- it will error. Do not call mine_kb, get_backlog, or backlog_update -- they are no-ops.

topic: Confidence value mapping -- Layer 2 to DB schema
content: CONFIDENCE MAPPING -- Layer 2 concepts to DB values: verified->proven, assumption_risk->low, user_corrected->medium, partial->medium. The knowledge_base table CHECK constraint only accepts: proven, high, medium, low. Never use verified, assumption_risk, or user_corrected as confidence values when calling add_knowledge or sb_insert on knowledge_base.

topic: Default behavior -- electrical engineering domain
content: DEFAULT BEHAVIOR -- electrical engineering domain. When asked about relay protection settings, IEC curve types, CT/PT ratios, coordination margins, or any protection relay parameter: do NOT answer from memory. Ask owner to confirm the specific values before treating them as fact. Pattern: state what is generally known about the topic, then ask: 'Can you confirm the [specific value] for this project?' Reason: incorrect relay settings can cause protection failures with real-world consequences. CORE has been corrected on assumed relay curve defaults before -- treat this entire domain as assumption_risk until owner confirms.

topic: Default behavior -- electrical equipment specs and ratings
content: DEFAULT BEHAVIOR -- electrical engineering equipment specs. Never present equipment ratings (kA interrupting capacity, voltage class, CT accuracy class, cable sizing, busbar ratings) from memory as fact for the JK1-2 project or any specific project. These values are project-specific and must come from: (1) project files in F:\Project\, (2) owner confirmation, or (3) the relevant IEC/ANSI standard cited explicitly. If answering from general knowledge, always prefix with: 'From general IEC practice -- confirm against your project spec:' Never assume Schneider, LS Electric, or other vendor default values apply without checking the actual component datasheet or BOM.

topic: Default behavior -- CORE architecture questions
content: DEFAULT BEHAVIOR -- CORE architecture questions. When asked about CORE's own tables, tools, functions, or architecture: always verify from system_map or operating_context.json before answering. Never state from memory that a table exists, a tool is active, or a function does X -- these change across versions. Pattern: (1) check system_map for live component status, (2) check operating_context.json for schema, (3) then answer. If system_map or Railway is down, state explicitly: 'Based on last known state -- verify when Railway recovers.'

topic: Default behavior -- CORE code changes and deployments
content: DEFAULT BEHAVIOR -- CORE code changes. Before suggesting or making any change to core_tools.py, core_train.py, or core_main.py: (1) read the current function from GitHub, (2) check mistakes table for known issues in that domain, (3) run core_py_validate after any push. Never suggest a code change based on memory of what the file contains -- the file evolves every session. After any push: wait 90s for Railway deploy, then run a health check before declaring success. Railway deploy status 'completed' means crash -- only 'success' means healthy.

topic: Default behavior -- agentic execution discipline
content: DEFAULT BEHAVIOR -- agentic execution. Before executing any multi-step task: (1) decompose into ordered steps and state them, (2) label each step as read-only / reversible / destructive, (3) if any step is destructive -- show plan and wait for owner confirmation before step 1. Proceed silently only when all steps are read-only or reversible AND the task is single-system. Never batch-execute destructive steps. After each write: read back to confirm it landed. On error: stop and diagnose before retrying -- do not retry blindly. After 2 failed attempts on same step: report to owner.

topic: Default behavior -- tool and table verification before use
content: DEFAULT BEHAVIOR -- tool and table verification. Never call a tool or query a table that is not confirmed active in system_map. Before any tool call: verify it appears in system_map with status=active. Before any sb_query or sb_insert: verify the table is in the active_tables list from operating_context.json and is not in the tombstone list. Tombstoned tables that must never be queried: playbook, memory, master_prompt, patterns, training_sessions, training_sessions_v2, training_flags, session_learning, agent_registry, knowledge_blocks, agi_mistakes, stack_registry, vault_logs, vault, backlog.

topic: Default behavior -- cost and efficiency principles
content: DEFAULT BEHAVIOR -- cost and efficiency. When executing tasks involving repeated tool calls: (1) batch what can be batched -- use sb_bulk_insert over multiple sb_inserts, use multi_patch over multiple gh_search_replace calls when Unicode is not involved, (2) avoid redundant reads -- if a file was already fetched this session, reuse it rather than re-fetching, (3) for Railway-dependent operations: group them to minimize round-trips, (4) for Groq calls: use GROQ_FAST (llama-3.1-8b-instant) for simple synthesis, GROQ_MODEL (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) for complex reasoning, (5) check resource_ceilings.json before heavy API work to avoid rate limit errors.

topic: Always validate syntax before pushing -- py_compile in every patch script
content: Always add a py_compile validation step in every patch script before pushing to GitHub. Pattern: after writing content to tmp file, run: import py_compile; py_compile.compile('C:/Users/rnvgg/tmp_file.py') -- raises SyntaxError before push if anything is broken. This prevents Railway crash loops. Also: never use multi-line f-strings with literal embedded newlines in patch scripts -- use escaped \\n inside single-line strings instead.

topic: Task 8 tools: patch_file, validate_syntax, append_to_file — when and how to use
content:
Three server-side patching tools added in Task 8 (2026-03-14). All run on Railway — never pull file content into Claude context.

TOOL SELECTION RULES:
1. patch_file  — use instead of multi_patch when editing .py files. Key difference: runs py_compile BEFORE pushing. If syntax error detected, returns ok=False and does NOT push. Prevents crash loops. Same patches= JSON array format as multi_patch.
2. validate_syntax — use BEFORE any deploy of a .py file to verify syntax. Returns ok=True + line count OR ok=False + exact error line. Does not push anything.
3. append_to_file — use when adding new functions to an existing file. Fetches file server-side, appends, runs py_compile if .py, pushes. Never fetches file into Claude context.

WHEN TO USE EACH:
- Editing existing code in a .py file → patch_file (replaces multi_patch for .py files)
- Checking if current live file has syntax issues → validate_syntax
- Adding new functions/blocks to end of file → append_to_file
- Editing non-.py files → multi_patch or gh_search_replace still fine

NEVER use multi_patch for .py files anymore — it has no syntax check. If multi_patch was the old default for .py patches, patch_file is now the correct tool.

dry_run="true" on patch_file returns diff without pushing — useful to preview changes before committing.

All three tools live in core_tools.py and are registered in TOOLS dict. They are available as MCP tools immediately after the Task 8 deploy.

topic: patch_file tool usage pattern
content:
patch_file takes: path (str), patches (JSON array string), message (str), repo (str, optional), dry_run (str "true"/"false").

patches format — same as multi_patch:
[{"old_str": "exact text to find", "new_str": "replacement text"}, ...]

Behavior:
1. Fetches file from GitHub server-side (no context cost)
2. Applies patches (skips not-found or ambiguous, same as multi_patch)
3. If path ends in .py: writes to temp file, runs python3 -m py_compile
4. If syntax error: returns {"ok": false, "error": "Syntax error - NOT pushed: ..."} — file unchanged on GitHub
5. If syntax ok (or non-.py): calls gh_write to push
6. Returns applied/skipped counts + syntax_ok flag

Use dry_run="true" to preview patches without pushing.

CRITICAL: This is the safe replacement for multi_patch on .py files. Always prefer patch_file over multi_patch for any Python file.

topic: append_to_file tool usage pattern
content:
append_to_file takes: path (str), content_to_append (str), message (str), repo (str, optional).

Behavior:
1. Fetches existing file from GitHub server-side
2. Concatenates: existing + content_to_append
3. If .py: runs py_compile on combined result before push
4. Returns original_lines, appended_lines, total_lines

Use case: adding new t_* functions to core_tools.py, adding config entries, extending any file without reading the whole thing into Claude's context window.

content_to_append must include leading newlines. Example:
"\n\ndef t_new_tool(arg: str) -> dict:\n    ..."

NOTE: Does not update the TOOLS dict automatically. After appending a new t_* function, still need a separate patch_file call to add the TOOLS entry.

topic: SESSION.md purpose and scope — what it is and is NOT
content:
SESSION.md (GitHub: pockiesaints7/core-agi/SESSION.md) is the STATIC OPERATING MANUAL for CORE. It contains:
- Session start checklist (Desktop vs web)
- What CORE is (identity paragraph)
- Current SOP (plan→execute→log→reflect→stop)
- Autonomous mode protocol
- Active rules table (tool rules, edge cases)
- A slim task history index (task names + done/pending, no subtasks)
- Incident log

SESSION.md does NOT contain:
- The full task registry with subtasks (that lives in Supabase task_queue)
- Current step / resume point (that lives in sessions table via session_end)
- Checkbox tracking (no more markdown ticking)

SESSION.md should NEVER grow due to normal session activity. It only changes when:
- A new permanent rule is added to Active Rules
- A new incident is logged
- The task history index needs a one-liner added

NEVER write subtask detail back to SESSION.md. NEVER tick checkboxes in SESSION.md. That system is retired.

topic: Task lifecycle — how to handle tasks at every stage of a session
content:
Task registry lives in Supabase table: task_queue, filtered by source=core_v6_registry.

EVERY SESSION START — run this query after session_start:
  sb_query(table="task_queue", filters="source=eq.core_v6_registry&status=eq.pending&order=priority.desc")
This returns all open tasks with full subtask JSON. Pick the highest priority non-blocked task to work on.

WHEN A NEW TASK ARRIVES (Ki defines it or synthesize_evolutions generates it):
  sb_insert(table="task_queue", data={
    "task": JSON.stringify({task_id, title, description, subtasks, blocked_by?}),
    "status": "pending",
    "priority": 1-10,
    "source": "core_v6_registry"
  })
Never write it to SESSION.md. The task lives in Supabase from day one.

WHEN A SUBTASK IS DONE:
  Read the full task row first (sb_query to get current JSON).
  Update the subtask status inside the task JSON.
  Patch via: sb_query is read-only — use gh_search_replace or note it in session_end summary.
  Actually: mark subtask done by updating the task JSON and doing sb_insert of updated row,
  OR just track it in session_end summary + hot reflection (Groq processes it).
  Simplest correct approach: note completed subtask in session_end actions field.

WHEN ALL SUBTASKS OF A TASK ARE DONE:
  Update status to done:
  The correct tool is sb_query with a PATCH — but sb_query is GET only.
  Use: core-agi:update_state(key="task_done", value="TASK-9", reason="all subtasks complete")
  OR use sb_insert of a sessions row noting the task completion.
  BEST approach: add a dedicated note in session_end completed_tasks field AND
  mentally flag for future: CORE needs a t_task_update tool (Task 12 candidate).

EVERY SESSION END:
  - session_end actions field must list what subtasks were completed this session
  - session_end new_step field must say which task + subtask is next
  - Do NOT update SESSION.md with task detail — session_end handles persistence

TASK STATUS VALUES: pending | in_progress | done | blocked

topic: task_queue schema and query patterns for core_v6_registry tasks
content:
task_queue table schema (relevant columns):
- id: uuid (auto)
- task: text — stores JSON with keys: task_id, title, description, subtasks (object or array), blocked_by (optional)
- status: text — "pending" | "in_progress" | "done" | "blocked" | "completed" | "waiting"
- priority: integer — 1 (low) to 10 (high)
- source: text — use "core_v6_registry" for all CORE v6 development tasks
- result: text — set when status=done, brief description of outcome
- created_at: timestamp (auto)
- updated_at: timestamp (auto)

CORE v6 registry query (all tasks):
  sb_query(table="task_queue", filters="source=eq.core_v6_registry&order=priority.desc&limit=20")

Open tasks only:
  sb_query(table="task_queue", filters="source=eq.core_v6_registry&status=eq.pending&order=priority.desc")

IMPORTANT: sb_query is READ-ONLY (GET). To update a task status, use sb_insert for a new row
OR wait for a t_task_update tool to be built (planned as part of CORE improvements).
Current workaround: track task completion in session_end summary + note in actions field.

topic: Groq capabilities and public source ingestion architecture
content:
Groq (llama-3.3-70b-versatile) capabilities and hard limits for CORE's background_researcher:

HARD LIMITS:
- NO internet access at runtime. Cannot fetch URLs, scrape GitHub, read docs, or access any live web content.
- Knowledge cutoff: December 2023. Knows nothing after that date from its own training.
- Text-only model. Cannot process images, audio, PDFs.
- Context window: 128K tokens — this is large and should be fully used.

WHAT GROQ CAN DO (given text in the prompt):
- Extract behavioral patterns from logs, mistakes, sessions
- Reason over pasted documentation and produce actionable output
- Identify gaps between current CORE state and best practices
- Generate evolution proposals, KB entries, tool designs
- Validate JSON output format reliably

THE CORRECT ARCHITECTURE for external source ingestion:
CORE's background_researcher (or Claude manually via web_fetch) fetches external content → pastes raw text into Groq prompt → Groq extracts patterns → output written to hot_reflections → cold processor picks up → evolutions proposed.

PRIORITY PUBLIC SOURCES (Claude fetches, Groq reasons over):
P0 — pockiesaints7/core-agi GitHub diffs (CORE's own evolution)
P0 — langchain-ai/langchain GitHub (agent/tool patterns)
P0 — openai/openai-cookbook GitHub (proven AGI orchestration patterns)
P1 — docs.anthropic.com (Claude tool use, system prompt best practices)
P1 — fastapi.tiangolo.com (CORE runs FastAPI)
P1 — supabase.com/changelog (CORE's brain is Supabase)
P2 — huggingface.co/papers (emerging agentic AI research)
P2 — railway.app docs (CORE's executor)

HOW TO ADD A SOURCE TO background_researcher:
In _run_simulation_batch() or a new _ingest_public_source() function:
1. httpx.get(url, timeout=15) to fetch the content
2. Trim to max 3000 chars (token budget)
3. Prepend to runtime_context before groq_chat() call
This pattern already works — just needs to be wired in.

topic: Full tool audit findings - v6 tools evolution session
content: During full audit of all 57 MCP tools in core_tools.py, the following critical architectural patterns were identified: (1) t_reflect is dead - zero callers in codebase, fully superseded by auto_hot_reflection called automatically by session_end. (2) t_project_get and t_project_prepare both crash when called via MCP because they split project_ids by comma internally, but _ARRAY_PARAMS sends them as a JSON array. (3) t_deploy_and_wait crashes on every completion with NameError: notify_owner not defined. (4) backlog table is a tombstone - queries fail, but t_bulk_apply still patches it. (5) t_multi_patch has no py_compile guard - can silently push broken Python. (6) t_get_mistakes only returns 4 of 7 fields, missing severity, root_cause, how_to_avoid. (7) t_add_knowledge never populates the instruction field - KB behavioral directives are always empty.

topic: core_tools.py indentation standard — 4 spaces, no tabs, read before write
content: core_tools.py uses 4-space indentation throughout. Function bodies: 4 spaces. Nested blocks: 8, 12 spaces etc. TOOLS registry: tool name key at 4 spaces indent, continuation desc line at 27 spaces to align with opening brace. No tabs anywhere. Always verify by reading gh_read_lines on the insertion area before writing any patch.

topic: HARD RULE: Read before write — always read insertion point before patching
content: HARD RULE — NO EXCEPTIONS: Before writing ANY code to a file, always call gh_read_lines on the exact lines surrounding the insertion point first. Read 5-10 lines around where the code will go. This is mandatory even if you just read the file earlier in the session — files change, mental models drift. Applies to: patch_file, gh_search_replace, multi_patch, append_to_file, or any GitHub write operation. The read takes 1 tool call and prevents indentation mismatches, wrong quote styles, context errors, and crash loops. Skipping this step is the root cause of most patching failures in CORE sessions.

topic: task_queue_status_update_at_session_end
instruction: Before calling session_end, always update the active task's status in task_queue via sb_patch or task_update. If task is partially complete → in_progress. If task is fully done → done. SESSION.md current step update is secondary — task_queue is the source of truth. Skipping this causes task status drift that persists across sessions.

topic: SESSION.md purpose and scope
instruction: SESSION.md is a cold-start bootstrap document ONLY — not a task registry, not a session log, not a task checklist. Its only job is to give Claude enough context to start when session_start fails or when opening from claude.ai with no MCP. It must contain: (1) last_good_commit for Railway recovery, (2) session start checklist for Desktop + claude.ai paths, (3) active rules table for hard tool conventions, (4) autonomous mode protocol for local PC paths, (5) one-paragraph WHAT IS CORE. Nothing else. Task registry lives in task_queue. Session history lives in sessions table. Checkbox theater (- [ ] TASK-X) is dead — never maintain it. Session log table in SESSION.md is a duplicate of sessions Supabase table — do not append to it.

topic: SESSION.md anti-patterns — what must never be added
instruction: Never add new sections to SESSION.md that duplicate Supabase data. If you find yourself writing task history, session logs, or checkbox lists into SESSION.md — stop. That data belongs in task_queue (tasks), sessions table (history), or knowledge_base (rules). SESSION.md sections that are dead weight and must be removed in cleanup: Section 6 TASK REGISTRY (emoji checklist), Section 7 SESSION LOG table. These diverge from Supabase the moment they are written and create false source-of-truth confusion.

topic: core_py_validate false positive patterns
instruction: When core_py_validate reports errors at lines inside t_core_py_validate itself (e.g. backboard.railway in string literals, odd triple-quote count), treat these as false positives. The validator scans its own check strings. Verify by running search_in_file — if all matches are inside the validator function, errors are self-referential and safe to ignore.
content: Confirmed false positives in TASK-13.H: L656-657 backboard.railway errors are inside the check string itself. Odd triple-quote count (121) is inflated by the validator counting its own content.count('\"\"\"') check string.

topic: task_queue patch must use UUID not LIKE filter
instruction: Always update task_queue rows by UUID (id=eq.{uuid}), never by task::text LIKE filter. LIKE '%TASK-N%' is not unique — TASK-13 matches TASK-13 v2 and TASK-3, TASK-4 matches TASK-14. Always query IDs first with sb_query select=id,task, then patch by exact UUID.

topic: supabase_pat
instruction: Supabase management API PAT. Use for POST https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/qbfaplqiakwjvrtwpbmr/database/query to list live DB tables. Fetch from KB at runtime in _reconcile_brain_tables() — do NOT hardcode in source files.
content: sbp_6459d1dadd24c7cbb53eaca710bc0345e3f44ad6

topic: management_api_endpoint
instruction: Use POST /v1/projects/{ref}/database/query for Supabase management API SQL queries. Fetch PAT from KB at runtime — never hardcode in source.
content: POST https://api.supabase.com/v1/projects/{ref}/database/query with header Authorization: Bearer {PAT} and body {query: SQL}. Returns array of row objects. PAT is stored in KB domain=system.config topic=supabase_pat. NOT: GET /database/tables (404). NOT: POST /db/query (404).

topic: deploy_sop
instruction: Deploy SOP: patch_file (pushes code) -> Railway auto-deploys -> sleep 35s -> build_status() to confirm. For manual redeploy (no code change): redeploy() -> sleep 35s -> build_status(). NEVER call deploy_and_wait -- it is deprecated.
content: Railway auto-deploys on every GitHub push. No polling loop needed. deploy_and_wait was a broken blocking loop -- it is now deprecated (calls build_status internally). redeploy() is only needed for manual force-redeploy with no code change.

topic: skill_file_location
instruction: Active CORE skill file lives at C:\Users\rnvgg\.claude-skills\CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md. When a new hard rule is learned, write it directly to this file using Windows-MCP:FileSystem, then tell REINVAGNAR to re-upload to Claude Desktop. Never output the content and ask owner to paste it.
content: Path: C:\Users\rnvgg\.claude-skills\CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md. Contains all 3 layers (Identity, Behavior Pipeline, Scan Graph). REINVAGNAR manually uploads this file to Claude Desktop as a skill attachment after any update. Claude writes updates directly to this file -- never asks owner. Owner re-uploads after any change for it to take effect next session.

topic: tool_priority_and_connections
instruction: Zapier is RANK 1 for all external service operations. It connects 8000+ apps and is the primary expansion vector for CORE autonomy. When a task requires reaching an external service -- check zapier:* tools FIRST before any other namespace. When a needed Zapier connection does not exist yet, explicitly request REINVAGNAR to add it via the config URL. Always know current Zapier connection state from ZAPIER_CONNECTIONS.md and ZAPIER_SKILL_GRAPH.md before deciding a tool is unavailable.
content: Zapier MCP config URL: https://mcp.zapier.com/mcp/servers/1b142a72-dda7-4341-a414-0a55cc6ee99f/config. Currently connected apps (from ZAPIER_SKILL_GRAPH.md): Gmail (12 tools), Google Calendar (10), Todoist (7), Webhooks (3), Google Drive (5), Google Docs (3), Google Sheets (3), Google Slides (2), GitHub via Zapier (19), Google AI Studio/Gemini (8). Total ~72 active Zapier tools. Domains NOT yet connected: Notion, Slack, Perplexity, PagerDuty, Better Uptime, OpenAI, Twitter/X, Stripe, WhatsApp, HubSpot. Full expansion plan in ZAPIER_CONNECTIONS.md.

topic: tool_namespace_priority
instruction: Tool namespace priority order: 1=zapier:* 2=core-agi:* 3=github:* 4=Desktop Commander:* 5=Filesystem:* 6=windows-mcp:* 7=Claude built-ins. Zapier is rank 1 for all external reach. core-agi is rank 1 only for CORE internal state. When a zapier connection is missing for a needed service, proactively request REINVAGNAR to add it via https://mcp.zapier.com/mcp/servers/1b142a72-dda7-4341-a414-0a55cc6ee99f/config
content: RANK 1: zapier:* -- all external services (Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Docs, Sheets, Gemini, GitHub non-core, Webhooks, Todoist, 8000+ apps). RANK 2: core-agi:* -- all CORE internal state (KB, mistakes, sessions, evolutions, Railway, Supabase). RANK 3: github:* direct -- complex GitHub ops, multi-file commits, Railway-down fallback. RANK 4: Desktop Commander:* -- local file write, background processes, long-running tasks. RANK 5: Filesystem:* -- local file read (simple). RANK 6: windows-mcp:* / Windows-MCP:* -- PowerShell, UI, registry (LOCAL ONLY). RANK 7: Claude built-ins -- web_search, web_fetch (when Zapier/Perplexity not available). Decision rule: Does task touch CORE internal state? -> core-agi. Does it reach external service/app? -> zapier FIRST. Complex GitHub/local file? -> github/Desktop Commander. Need new Zapier connection? -> REQUEST it from REINVAGNAR explicitly.

topic: missing_connection_protocol
instruction: When a task needs an external service not yet in Zapier, proactively request REINVAGNAR to add it. Never silently fall back to a worse tool without flagging the missing connection. State exactly which app + which actions to enable + why CORE needs it.
content: Protocol: When CORE needs an external service that is not yet connected in Zapier, explicitly tell REINVAGNAR: "I need [App Name] connected in Zapier to do [task]. Please add [Action Names] at https://mcp.zapier.com/mcp/servers/1b142a72-dda7-4341-a414-0a55cc6ee99f/config". Do not silently fail or use a worse tool -- always surface the gap and request the connection. Priority order for what to request next is documented in ZAPIER_CONNECTIONS.md (Week 1 P0 list: Notion, Slack, Perplexity, Better Uptime, PagerDuty, OpenAI).

topic: deploy_and_wait deprecated — Railway auto-deploys on push, use build_status after 35s wait
content: Railway auto-deploys on every GitHub push — deploy_and_wait polling loop is redundant and broken by design. The deploy container is killed mid-poll when a new commit triggers Railway. Always use: patch_file (pushes code) then wait 35s then build_status() to confirm state. For no-code redeploys: redeploy() then 35s then build_status(). Never combine trigger + poll in one call. Exceptions: none.

topic: read_before_patch_rule
instruction: Always call gh_read_lines on the exact target lines before writing ANY patch. Copy indentation exactly from the live file. Never write indentation from memory or a mental model of the file.
content: Before writing any patch to a source file, always call gh_read_lines on the exact lines surrounding the insertion point. Copy indentation character-for-character from the live file. Mental models of file indentation drift across long sessions and cause silent patch failures or syntax errors. This is non-negotiable even for small single-line edits. The file may have changed since last read. Exceptions: none.

topic: cold_reflections_audit_trail
instruction: After every cold processor run, write a cold_reflections row via sb_post_critical. Do this even if evolutions_queued=0. The row is the audit trail. Never let it fail silently.
content: The cold processor must always write a cold_reflections row after every run, even if zero evolutions were queued. This row is the audit trail proving the pipeline executed. Use sb_post_critical to bypass rate limiter -- never let this fail silently. If a cold_reflections row is missing, the pipeline ran blind and the session learning was lost. Exceptions: none.

topic: synthesize_evolutions -- Claude is architect, not Groq
instruction: When owner says "synthesize" in Desktop chat, call synthesize_evolutions tool. It returns all signals (pending evolutions, top patterns, cold themes, hot gaps, open tasks). YOU (Claude) then read everything and act as architect -- think 6 months ahead, identify what CORE needs, call task_add for each new task. Do NOT delegate this reasoning to Groq. Groq handles data gathering (hot reflection, cold clustering). Claude handles blueprint and task registration.

topic: patch SOP -- gh_read_lines before every patch, TOOLS dict after every refactor
instruction: MANDATORY PATCH SOP -- enforce before every patch_file or gh_search_replace call, no exceptions: (1) core_py_fn to understand the function, (2) gh_read_lines on the EXACT target line range to get character-perfect content, (3) copy old_str verbatim from step 2 output -- never write from memory, (4) patch_file, (5) gh_read_lines again to verify lines landed correctly, (6) build_status to confirm deploy. After ANY function refactor: search TOOLS dict for the function name, read exact lines of the entry, patch the description in the same session. Never close a session with a stale TOOLS dict entry.
content: Violating this causes silent patch skips, requiring extra round trips. root_cause: core_py_fn output shows function body but surrounding block indentation may differ. old_str written from memory never matches 100%.

topic: AGI Rule #1 -- evolution must write to persistent storage, not chat promises
instruction: RULE 1 FOR AGI: EVOLVE = DATA IN STORAGE NOT CHAT. Every new rule must hit ALL THREE: (1) knowledge_base via add_knowledge, (2) SESSION.md Active Rules table via gh_search_replace, (3) CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md at C:\Users\rnvgg\.claude-skills\CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md via Windows-MCP:FileSystem. NOTE: only CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md is local-PC-only -- all other CORE files (source code, SESSION.md, docs) go to GitHub normally. Until TASK-21: do all 3 manually. After TASK-21: add_evolution_rule() handles 1+2, Windows-MCP handles 3.
content: Chat promises die with session. New Claude instance has zero memory. Persistence = Supabase tables + GitHub SESSION.md + local skill file. Skill file local-only is a FEATURE -- means only REINVAGNAR machine runs CORE. TASK-21 builds add_evolution_rule tool + session_end gate to enforce all 3 writes before close.

topic: Rule 22: mandatory patch SOP -- gh_read_lines before every patch
instruction: MANDATORY PATCH SOP -- zero exceptions: (1) core_py_fn to understand function, (2) gh_read_lines on EXACT target lines, (3) copy old_str CHARACTER-FOR-CHARACTER from step 2 -- never from memory, (4) patch_file, (5) gh_read_lines to verify landed, (6) build_status to confirm deploy. Writing old_str from memory is forbidden -- causes silent skips requiring extra round trips.

topic: Rule 23: mandatory TOOLS dict update after every function refactor
instruction: After ANY function behavior change, ALWAYS update the TOOLS dict entry in the SAME session: (1) gh_read_lines around the function's TOOLS entry (line ~2400+ in core_tools.py), (2) read exact desc and perm fields, (3) patch_file to update desc + perm. Never close a session with a stale TOOLS dict description. Stale descriptions mislead future sessions about what a tool does.

topic: Rule 28: deploy SOP -- patch_file then build_status, deploy_and_wait deprecated
instruction: Deploy SOP: patch_file pushes code -> Railway auto-deploys -> wait 35s -> build_status() to confirm. deploy_and_wait is DEPRECATED -- never use it. For no-code redeploy: redeploy() -> wait 35s -> build_status(). Never combine redeploy trigger + poll in one call -- Railway kills the container mid-poll.

topic: Rule 29: synthesize_evolutions is Claude's job -- Claude is architect not Groq
instruction: synthesize_evolutions is CLAUDE'S job. When owner says synthesize: call the tool, it returns all signals (pending evolutions, top patterns, cold themes, hot gaps, open tasks). YOU read everything in Desktop chat, act as unconstrained architect thinking 6 months ahead, then call task_add for each new task directly. Groq owns hot_reflection + cold_processor. Claude owns synthesis and blueprint. No Groq in synthesis. No auto task insertion.

topic: Rule TASK-21.B: session_end skill_file_updated gate
instruction: session_end TASK-21.B gate: if patterns field is non-empty, you MUST write new rules to C:\Users\rnvgg\.claude-skills\CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md via Windows-MCP:FileSystem FIRST, then call session_end with skill_file_updated=true. Gate blocks if patterns present and skill_file_updated=false. Override with force_close=true (owner explicit skip only). Flow: add_knowledge + gh_search_replace SESSION.md + Windows-MCP write to skill file -> session_end skill_file_updated=true.

topic: Rules 5-8: sb_query filters, read_file repo, read-back, tombstone tables
instruction: Never use query_string in sb_query -- use filters param only. Never pass repo arg to core-agi:read_file or write_file -- default is correct. Always read back after every write to confirm it landed. Never query tombstone tables -- check system_map status first.

topic: Rule 9: task_queue is single task registry -- always query at session start
instruction: task_queue (source=core_v6_registry) is the ONLY task registry. SESSION.md does NOT track tasks. At every session start: sb_query task_queue filters=source=eq.core_v6_registry&status=eq.pending&order=priority.desc. Pick highest priority non-blocked task. Never start work without querying task_queue.

topic: Rules 13-16: Railway down fallback, PowerShell local only, changelog, credentials
instruction: changelog_add after EVERY deployment or structural change. Before any auth task: read CREDENTIALS.md first -- never ask owner, never guess credentials. When Railway is down: github:* only -- NEVER retry core-agi:* tools, they all route through Railway and will all fail. PowerShell is LOCAL ONLY -- never touches Supabase, GitHub, Railway, or any external API.

topic: Rules 21+24: append_to_file restrictions and multi_patch forbidden on .py files
instruction: append_to_file is ONLY safe for helper functions NOT referenced in the TOOLS registry. The TOOLS dict is in the MIDDLE of core_tools.py -- a function appended to the end is defined AFTER the TOOLS dict references it, causing NameError crash on Railway startup. Use patch_file to insert functions BEFORE the TOOLS block. NEVER use multi_patch on .py files -- it has no py_compile guard. One bad edit crashes Railway.

topic: Rules 25-27: skill file local PC only, write directly, never GitHub
instruction: CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md is LOCAL PC ONLY at C:\Users\rnvgg\.claude-skills\CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md. REINVAGNAR uploads it manually to Claude Desktop. NEVER push to GitHub. NEVER make public. This rule applies ONLY to this specific file -- all other CORE files (source code, SESSION.md, docs) go to GitHub normally. Write updates via Windows-MCP:FileSystem or Desktop Commander:edit_block. Never ask owner to manually edit it.

topic: battle_tested_debug_standard
instruction: When asked to debug any CORE function: use t_debug_fn(fn_name, dry_run=True) first. It runs staged execution and returns exactly which stage failed. Never guess root cause from a silent False return. Always reproduce EXACT runtime conditions, not a simplified approximation.
content:
CORE DEBUG DOCTRINE:

WHAT MAKES A DEBUG ENDPOINT BATTLE-TESTED:
1. STAGE LABELS -- 0_resolve, 1_preflight, 2_execute, 4_write. First failed stage = exact failure point.
2. DRY RUN DEFAULT -- dry_run=True intercepts all DB writes. Never debug with live writes unless intentional.
3. RAW OUTPUT EXPOSED -- return raw LLM response BEFORE parsing. Most silent failures are JSON parse errors.
4. INPUT ECHO -- return what was actually sent (prompt length, data counts). Spots prompt bloat or missing context.
5. FULL TRACEBACK -- traceback.format_exc(), not str(e). str(e) hides the line number.
6. WRITE RESULT EXPLICIT -- confirm whether write succeeded, was skipped (dry_run), or failed.
7. NEVER SWALLOW -- outer except must RETURN the error, not print to stdout. Stdout is invisible from Claude.

WHY /debug/sim FAILED INITIALLY:
- v1: called _run_simulation_batch() as black box -- returned ok=False, no detail
- v2: called groq_chat with simplified inputs -- worked, hiding the real issue
- v3 (battle-tested): reproduced full runtime_context + intercepted sb_post -- found actual failure

LESSON: Debug must reproduce EXACT runtime conditions, not a simplified approximation.

MCP TOOL: t_debug_fn(fn_name, dry_run=True) -- in TOOLS dict, callable from Claude Desktop.
HTTP ENDPOINTS: GET /debug/sim, GET /debug/real -- for quick browser/curl testing.
FUTURE: Every new function in core_train.py and core_tools.py should have a corresponding debug registration in t_debug_fn known_staged dict.

topic: patch_tool_architecture
instruction: ALL patch tools (gh_search_replace, multi_patch, patch_file, append_to_file) now use _gh_blob_read (read) and _gh_blob_write (write). NEVER revert to gh_read/gh_write for these tools.
content:
ROOT CAUSES OF HIGH FAIL RATE ON LARGE FILE PATCHES:

1. SHA CONFLICT (was the #1 cause) -- gh_write (Contents API PUT) requires a current file SHA. Fetch SHA via GET, then PUT. If anything wrote to the file between GET and PUT (another patch, deploy, concurrent write), GitHub returns 409 Conflict. gh_write returns is_success=False. No retry. Patch silently lost. FIXED: all patch tools now use _gh_blob_write (Git Trees API) which creates an atomic commit with no SHA dependency -- 409 is impossible.

2. WHITESPACE "NOT FOUND" -- old_str match was pure string equality. One \r\n vs \n, trailing space, or tab vs space = "not found" with zero explanation. FIXED: _patch_find() helper tries exact match first, then line-ending-normalized fallback (\r\n->\n), then returns character-level ndiff hint showing exactly what differs. Same approach as Desktop Commander edit_block.

3. gh_read (Contents API) truncates large files -- Contents API has a 1MB limit and may return truncated content for files near the limit. _gh_blob_read (Blobs API) has no size limit and always returns the complete file. FIXED: all reads now use _gh_blob_read.

4. WHY edit_block IS MORE RELIABLE -- operates on local filesystem, no network, no SHA, no encoding. Character-level diff shown on near-miss. Our tools now have equivalent fallback logic.

ARCHITECTURE AFTER FIX:
- gh_search_replace: _gh_blob_read + _patch_find + _gh_blob_write
- multi_patch: _gh_blob_read + _patch_find (per patch) + _gh_blob_write
- patch_file: _gh_blob_read + _patch_find (per patch) + py_compile + _gh_blob_write
- append_to_file: _gh_blob_read + _gh_blob_write
- validate_syntax: _gh_blob_read only (no write)

_patch_find() returns: (found, count, matched_str, hint)
  hint = None (exact match) | "line_ending_normalized" | "near_miss: <ndiff>"

topic: evolution_rule: At boot, NEVER declare any tool available or unavailable based on interface name
instruction: At boot, NEVER declare any tool available or unavailable based on interface name (claude.ai vs Desktop). Tool availability is determined solely by which MCP servers are connected to the session. Mandatory boot SOP: (1) tool_search for Windows-MCP + Desktop Commander, (2) make one live test call each, (3) report only what the test confirms. No assumptions. No inference from interface type.
content: category=hard_rule source=owner_correction_2026-03-15. Established via add_evolution_rule. Persists across all sessions.

topic: evolution_rule: Before writing ANY patch old_str, ALWAYS call gh_read_lines on the exact target
instruction: Before writing ANY patch old_str, ALWAYS call gh_read_lines on the exact target lines first and copy the string character-for-character from the read output. Never write old_str from memory or from a previous read in the same session — the file may have changed. A near-miss hint showing leading space differences means the indentation was assumed, not read.
content: category=hard_rule source=session_pattern_2026-03-15. Established via add_evolution_rule. Persists across all sessions.

topic: evolution_rule: When diagnosing any broken CORE function: never simplify the inputs to make it e
instruction: When diagnosing any broken CORE function: never simplify the inputs to make it easier to test. A debug harness that uses a simplified prompt or reduced context will pass while the real function fails — because the failure is in the runtime context, not the logic. Always reproduce the exact runtime context in the debug call. debug_fn dry_run=true is the correct tool for this.
content: category=hard_rule source=session_pattern_2026-03-15. Established via add_evolution_rule. Persists across all sessions.

topic: evolution_rule: When a tool or API call fails with an auth or permission error, test the API beh
instruction: When a tool or API call fails with an auth or permission error, test the API behavior directly before assuming the credential is wrong. The Railway GraphQL schema was accessible without any token — meaning the missing piece was the projectId argument, not a new PAT. Always test with a minimal API call first. Never request new credentials before verifying the existing ones with a live test.
content: category=hard_rule source=session_pattern_2026-03-15. Established via add_evolution_rule. Persists across all sessions.

topic: evolution_rule: Never write patch old_str from memory. SOP: gh_read_lines(start, end) → copy exa
instruction: Never write patch old_str from memory. SOP: gh_read_lines(start, end) → copy exact old_str character-for-character → patch_file. No exceptions, even for single-line edits. The near-miss hint (- old + new) is the signal that memory was used instead of reading. Violating this causes skipped patches and requires extra round-trips.
content: category=hard_rule source=session_pattern_2026-03-15. Established via add_evolution_rule. Persists across all sessions.

topic: evolution_rule: debug_fn must reproduce the exact runtime context, not a simplified version. v1
instruction: debug_fn must reproduce the exact runtime context, not a simplified version. v1 (ok:false, error:null) = insufficient. v2 (simplified Groq call) = hides real issue. v3 (exact runtime context + sb_post intercepted) = correct. A debug endpoint that uses a different code path than the real function is not a debug endpoint — it's a green test on a different problem.
content: category=sop source=session_pattern_2026-03-15. Established via add_evolution_rule. Persists across all sessions.

topic: evolution_rule: Never assume an API failure is a credential problem before testing the API behav
instruction: Never assume an API failure is a credential problem before testing the API behavior directly. Railway GraphQL schema was publicly introspectable without auth — the failure was missing projectId, not a wrong token. SOP: make a minimal live API call first, read the actual error, then diagnose. Jumping to "need a new credential" wastes time and creates unnecessary secrets.
content: category=hard_rule source=session_pattern_2026-03-15. Established via add_evolution_rule. Persists across all sessions.

topic: evolution_rule: When constructing old_str for any patch: use the raw field from gh_read_lines, N
instruction: When constructing old_str for any patch: use the raw field from gh_read_lines, NOT the content field. content is a numbered display format (4-digit prefix + 2 spaces) -- copying it corrupts old_str. raw contains exact file bytes. SOP: gh_read_lines → copy response.raw → use as old_str. If patch still fails after using raw, _patch_find has 5 normalization fallback tiers (line endings, trailing whitespace, tabs, combined) before returning not-found.
content: category=hard_rule source=owner_correction_2026-03-15. Established via add_evolution_rule. Persists across all sessions.

topic: interface detection — Claude Desktop vs claude.ai web
instruction: At every session boot, determine interface using this exact logic: IF any MCP tool call succeeds (Windows-MCP, Desktop Commander, core-agi, etc.) → interface = Claude Desktop app. IF no MCP tools are available at all → interface = claude.ai web. NEVER infer interface from the system prompt text or assumptions. The rule is: MCP reachable = Desktop app. No MCP = web.
content: Simple detection rule: if ANY MCP server is connected and callable, the user is on the Claude Desktop app — because MCP server connections require the Desktop app's MCP config. claude.ai web cannot configure or connect MCP servers. Therefore: MCP tools present = Claude Desktop app, always. Do not say "claude.ai web" when MCP tools are live. This has been a repeated mistake — treat this rule as hard, no exceptions.

topic: interface detection hard rule
instruction: HARD RULE at boot: MCP callable = Claude Desktop app. No MCP = claude.ai web. Simple. No exceptions.
content: IF any MCP tool is callable (core-agi, Windows-MCP, Desktop Commander, or any other MCP server) → user is on Claude Desktop app. FULL STOP. claude.ai web cannot connect MCP servers. This single check replaces all interface guessing. Wrong answer 5+ times by ignoring this. The fix is: stop inferring, start detecting. One tool call succeeds = Desktop app confirmed.

topic: writing Unicode to local files — correct tool
instruction: NEVER use Windows-MCP:PowerShell string manipulation to write Unicode characters (arrows →, em-dashes —, etc.) to local files. Always use Desktop Commander:edit_block or Desktop Commander:write_file. PowerShell mangles Unicode. Desktop Commander handles encoding correctly. This has failed 50+ times.
content: When writing to local skill files (.md, .txt, any text file on the PC): use Desktop Commander:edit_block for surgical edits, Desktop Commander:write_file for full rewrites. PowerShell Set-Content and -replace with Unicode strings strips or corrupts special characters (→, —, etc.) regardless of -Encoding UTF8. The only reliable local file writer is Desktop Commander. This applies to CORE_AGI_SKILL_V4.md and all other local skill files.

LSEI SCOPE — STRICTLY LIMITED TO:

- MLTX (Mold/Cast Resin Transformer) — units 1MLTX_B, 1MLTX_C, 1MLTX_D, 1MLTX_F

OUT OF SCOPE FOR LSEI — DO NOT include in daily reports or any LSEI documents:
- CCTV (not LSEI scope)

- UPS, Generator, any other MCE equipment
- Any other systems not explicitly listed above

When drafting or reviewing any LSEI document for this project, only reference RMU and MLTX activities. Any mention of CCTV, DCOS, UPS, or other systems = WRONG scope.
